title: Birth Injuries
authors: Prazad PA, Noble Rajpal M, Mangurten HH / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Birth Injuries?
Birth injuries บางชนิดดูไม่ร้ายแรง แต่ซ่อน mortality ไว้สูงมาก และหลายอย่างต้อง identify ภายในชั่วโมงแรก ไม่งั้น fatal. (1) Subgaleal hemorrhage: mortality 14% — subgaleal space จุ neonatal blood volume ทั้งหมด (>250 mL term baby) → hypovolemic shock ได้ไม่มี external signs ชัดเจน; (2) Subcutaneous fat necrosis → hypercalcemia 3–4 wk หลังคลอด (Ca up to 17.3 mg/dL) — ถ้า miss → CNS + renal damage; (3) Brachial plexus palsy: ถ้าไม่ refer ก่อน 6 เดือน → permanent deficit (surgery window เปิดแค่ช่วงนั้น); (4) Intra-abdominal injury: นิยมมา present เป็น shock + abdominal distension โดยไม่มี external blood loss — ต้อง suspect เสมอใน breech/macrosomic/hepatomegaly; (5) Document ทุก birth injury ก่อน discharge — ไม่งั้นอาจถูกสงสัย child abuse ภายหลัง. Birth injuries ลดลงมากหลัง 1981 (23.8 → 3.7 deaths/100,000 live births) แต่ยังเป็น cause of NICU admission ที่สำคัญ โดยเฉพาะ severe intracranial injuries after operative vaginal delivery.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
แยก caput succedaneum vs cephalhematoma vs subgaleal hemorrhage: anatomy, crossing suture lines, timing, risk
รู้ emergency management ของ subgaleal hemorrhage: clinical signs, 'silent presentation', volume resuscitation
อธิบาย subcutaneous fat necrosis: etiology, delayed hypercalcemia 3–4 wk, treatment (furosemide, bisphosphonate)
จำแนก brachial plexus palsy: Erb-Duchenne (C5-C6) vs Klumpke (C8-T1) vs total arm — clinical signs, DDx, surgery criteria
ระบุ phrenic nerve paralysis: etiology, CXR findings, treatment (plication ก่อน vs หลัง 30 วัน)
รู้ skeletal injuries: clavicle (most common fracture), humerus, femur — treatment + prognosis
อธิบาย intra-abdominal birth injuries: liver (most common), spleen, adrenal — presentation, imaging, surgical indication
ระบุ spinal cord injury: 4 clinical patterns, MRI prediction, supportive management (bladder/bowel/skin)
รู้ facial nerve palsy: peripheral vs central, Möbius DDx, eye protection, prognosis (90% spontaneous recovery)
เชื่อม birth injury risk factors กับ predisposing clinical scenarios: macrosomia, breech, vacuum, forceps, shoulder dystocia
1. ภาพรวม Birth Injuries — Risk Factors และ Trends
1.1 Risk Factors

Risk Factors ที่ทำให้ทารกเสี่ยง Birth Injury
Macrosomia; prematurity (bones + vessels fragile); cephalopelvic disproportion; dystocia + prolonged labor; abnormal presentation (breech, face, brow); operative delivery: vacuum extraction, forceps (mid-forceps highest risk); failed operative vaginal delivery → แล้วต้องทำ CS. การบาดเจ็บ 'avoidable' บางส่วน (เทคนิคผิด) แต่บางส่วน 'unavoidable' แม้ obstetric care ดีที่สุด.
Birth injury deaths: 1981 = 23.8/100,000 live births (rank 6th) → 1993 = 3.7/100,000 (rank 11th) → ปัจจุบัน ไม่อยู่ใน 10 leading causes แล้ว
สาเหตุที่ลดลง: refinement ของ obstetric techniques + เพิ่ม CS rate แทน difficult vaginal delivery
แต่ยังสำคัญ: เป็น NICU admission cause + source of significant morbidity โดยเฉพาะ severe intracranial injuries after operative vaginal delivery
1.2 หลักการสำคัญในการจัดการ
Thorough physical exam ทุก newborn: especially after difficult delivery, macrosomic infant, maternal motor vehicle collision
Document ก่อน discharge: ป้องกัน inappropriate suspicion of inflicted injury (child abuse) ภายหลัง
Differential diagnosis กว้าง: หลาย birth injuries เลียนแบบ medical conditions — ต้องรู้ DDx
2. Soft Tissue Injuries
2.1 Erythema, Abrasions, Petechiae, Ecchymoses
Erythema/abrasions: บริเวณ presenting part (dystocia) หรือ site of forceps application (linear, bilateral) — clean to prevent infection; resolves days

Petechiae: เกิดจาก ↑ intrathoracic + venous pressure ขณะ passage ผ่าน birth canal — บ่อยใน breech, precipitous delivery, tight nuchal cord. DDx: hemorrhagic disorder (platelet, DIC, infection) → check platelet, distribution. Localized + normal platelet = traumatic → ไม่ต้องรักษา, fades 2–3 days
Ecchymoses: ↑ ใน preterm + rapid labor; extensive → ↑ bilirubin (reabsorption) → phototherapy ± exchange; rarely shock
2.2 Subcutaneous Fat Necrosis — ระวัง Delayed Hypercalcemia

Subcutaneous Fat Necrosis — Clinical Pattern + Hypercalcemia Alert
Etiology: perinatal asphyxia (most common); therapeutic hypothermia; prostaglandin E; obstetric trauma; gestational DM; macrosomia; cold exposure. Mechanism: vascular compromise → localized fat necrosis → granulomatous inflammation with needle-shaped cholesterol crystals. Clinical: appears day 6–10 (or 2nd day to 6th week); sites: cheeks, back, shoulders, buttocks, thighs; hard, plaquelike, nonpitting, 1–10 cm, well-circumscribed; overlying skin colorless/red/purple. DANGER — Delayed Hypercalcemia 3–4 wk: extrarenal production of 1,25(OH)₂D by granulomatous cells → Ca up to 17.3 mg/dL → vomiting, weight loss, somnolence, irritability, seizures. Treatment: IV hydration + furosemide (calcium-wasting diuretic) + potassium citrate (inhibit renal stone) + corticosteroids; bisphosphonates (pamidronate/etidronate) for severe cases (Samedi Am J Perinatol 2014). Monitor serum Ca closely first 6 wk.
Differential: lipogranulomatosis, sclerema neonatorum (grave prognosis), nodular nonsuppurative panniculitis (fever + hepatosplenomegaly)
Treatment: observation only; surgical management for extensive ulcerated lesions only. Lesions soften 6–8 wk, regress within months. Residual: minimal atrophy ± small calcified areas
2.3 Lacerations (Accidental — Cesarean Section)
Scalpel laceration ระหว่าง CS: บริเวณ scalp, buttocks, thighs — superficial: adhesive strips; deeper: suture (finest material); rarely blood loss severe enough to suture in delivery room
Healing rapid; complications rare. Leave uncovered unless perineal area
3. Head Injuries — Scalp Hemorrhages

rows: NO — limited by periosteal attachments to one bone
Not apparent for hours or days

blocks.text: 3.1 Caput Succedaneum
Soft swelling few mm thick, may have overlying petechiae/ecchymoses — present at vertex delivery; resolves within days
Imaging if: large caput not diminishing by 48–72h; enlarging >24h after delivery; neurologic deficits; hemodynamic instability → rule out iatrogenic encephalocele (rare vacuum complication)
Treatment: ไม่ต้องรักษา; rarely hemorrhagic caput → shock → transfusion
3.2 Cephalhematoma
Incidence: 0.2–3% live births; ↑ primiparous mothers; parietal bones most common (unilateral); may be bilateral
Subperiosteal bleeding: slow → swelling not apparent for hours/days; palpable ridge of organizing tissue (may feel like central bony depression — artifact). May be fluctuant

Complications: (1) skull fracture (linear in small %; depressed → neurosurg consult); (2) infected cephalhematoma → sudden enlargement + fever → sepsis/meningitis relapse → diagnostic aspiration; (3) hyperkalemia + hyperbilirubinemia from resolving hematoma; (4) osteomyelitis → CT best for bone erosion; (5) venous sinus thrombosis + cerebellar hemorrhage → MRI
Routine incision/aspiration CONTRAINDICATED (infection risk). Treatment: observation; 80% resolve 3–4 wk; calcification → persistent up to years (Schüller's cephalhematoma deformans)
Linear fracture follow-up: radiograph 4–6 wk to exclude leptomeningeal cyst formation
3.3 Subgaleal Hemorrhage — Most Dangerous Scalp Injury

Subgaleal Hemorrhage — Emergency Recognition
Space: galea aponeurotica to periosteum — extremely expansive; extends entire scalp; can hold entire neonatal blood volume (≥250 mL term baby) → hypovolemic shock + DIC + multiorgan failure. Mortality: ~14% (Swanson Acta Obstet Gynecol 2012). Risk: vacuum extraction (silicone cup > metallic cup); male sex; macrosomia; coagulopathy; prematurity; cephalopelvic disproportion. Early signs: pallor, hypotonia, diffuse scalp swelling → fluctuant mass straddling sutures and fontanelles → pitting edema + ecchymosis + ear displaced anteriorly + periorbital swelling. CRITICAL — 'Silent Presentation': fluctuant mass absent initially despite serial exams — consider diagnosis in any infant with hypoperfusion + ↓ Hct after vacuum delivery even without palpable mass. Treatment: PROMPT blood volume restoration (FFP or blood); neurosurgery as last resort (bicoronal incision, bipolar cauterization, drain).
4. Skull Fractures
4.1 Types และ Clinical Features

Linear fracture (most common): ตาม forceps delivery หรือ prolonged labor (skull hits symphysis pubis/sacral promontory). Often with cephalhematoma. Infant usually normal behavior unless associated concussion/hemorrhage. Seldom diagnosed without radiograph. Treatment: no treatment needed for uncomplicated linear fractures

Depressed fracture ('ping-pong'): palpable/visible indentation; may be asymptomatic. Small (<5 mm): observe → 90% spontaneous elevation in 6 months. Nonsurgical elevation: breast pump vacuum, obstetric vacuum extractor. Surgical: large + neurologic signs, large depression, failure of nonsurgical elevation, CSF beneath galea

Basal fracture: separation of basal + squamous occipital bone → disruption of venous sinuses → shock + neurologic abnormalities + CSF from ears/nose → prognosis almost always fatal; survivors: extremely high neurologic sequelae
CT imaging: optimal if skull fracture + possible underlying injury suspected
5. Facial, Eye, and Cranial Nerve Injuries
5.1 Facial Nerve Palsy

Facial Nerve Palsy — Central vs Peripheral
Peripheral (most common birth injury): forceps compression near stylomastoid foramen or at mandibular ramus → flaccid paralysis entire ipsilateral face; open eyelid at rest (orbicular paralysis); mouth drawn to normal side with crying; smooth forehead on involved side. Feeding unaffected (tongue not involved). Central: spastic, limited to lower half/two-thirds of CONTRALATERAL face; normal forehead + eyelid movement; associated 6th nerve palsy common. DDx: Möbius syndrome (nuclear agenesis) = bilateral, expressionless, immobile face + other cranial nerve palsies + ear/palate/mandible anomalies. Absence/hypoplasia of depressor muscle of mouth angle (associated ↑ other congenital anomalies). Treatment: most: no therapy. Complete peripheral palsy: eye pad + artificial tears q4h (corneal protection). Prognosis: 90% complete spontaneous recovery (Falco Plast Reconstr Surg 1990); surgery considered after 1 year if no recovery; electrodiagnostics guide prognosis.
5.2 Eye Injuries
Subconjunctival hemorrhage: common; even after uncomplicated delivery; venous pressure effect; absorbed 1–2 wk; reassure parents

Retinal hemorrhage: compression of fetal head → venous congestion; flame-shaped near disk; 2–3x more common primiparous + forceps/vacuum; rare after CS. Most resolve 2 wk (some up to 6 wk). MUST document to avoid child abuse suspicion

Corneal haziness: usually edema from birth process; resolves 7–10 days. Persistent → Descemet membrane rupture from forceps malpositioning → leukoma/white opacity → amblyopia + strabismus → early refractive correction + amblyopia therapy
6th nerve (abducens) palsy: most common cranial nerve injured (long intracranial course); internal strabismus; mild → resolves 1–2 months

Horner syndrome: cervical sympathetic nerve trauma (usually with lower brachial plexus injury): miosis + partial ptosis + apparent enophthalmos + anhidrosis ipsilateral face; iris pigmentation delayed months
5.3 Nasal Septal Dislocation
Cartilaginous septum displaced from vomerine groove — intrauterine pressure or delivery pressure on nose
Compress nose tip test: septum dislocated → nostrils collapse + deviation apparent; normal → no deviation (Daily Mo Med 1977)
Respiratory distress (edema + narrow nasal passages) even without dislocation — avoid nasal suctioning/transnasal bronchoscopy until normal nasal ventilation re-established; pulse oximetry monitoring
Treatment: septal elevator → audible click = repositioned; Steri-Strip method for positional deformity without distress. Facial bone fractures unite quickly (7–10 days) → prompt attention
5.4 Vocal Cord Paralysis

Unilateral: excessive traction on head (breech) or lateral traction forceps; left recurrent laryngeal more often (lower origin, longer course). Hoarseness + mild inspiratory stridor with crying; may have feeding difficulty. Resolution: 4–6 wk without treatment

Bilateral: usually CNS insult (hypoxia/hemorrhage brainstem), not peripheral trauma. Dyspnea, retractions, stridor, cyanosis → may need immediate intubation → tracheostomy. Funnel deformity lower sternum if untreated (appears by day 15). Prognosis variable: 50% partial recovery within months
Flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy at bedside: facilitates diagnosis without NICU transport
6. Brachial Plexus Palsy
6.1 Classification

rows: Arm: adducted, internally rotated, elbow extended, pronated forearm, flexed wrist ('waiter's tip'). Absent Moro/biceps/radial reflex. Grasp intact
Hand paralyzed; wrist/finger flexors lost; grasp absent; DT reflexes intact; Horner syndrome ipsilateral; ulnar sensory deficit
Complete flaccid arm; all reflexes absent; sensory deficit to shoulder

blocks.text: 6.2 Etiology และ Mechanism

Most: prolonged + difficult labor → macrosomic, asphyxiated infant. Upper arm: breech with arms over head → excessive traction on shoulder; vertex → lateral traction head/neck away from shoulder (shoulder dystocia, vacuum)
Nerve injury types: nerve sheath torn + hemorrhage/edema (most common), complete rupture, root avulsion from spinal cord
'No shoulder' brachial plexus palsy: intrauterine insult pre-labor (uterine tumor, maternal pelvic bony prominence) — not always delivery traction
Associated injuries: phrenic nerve palsy (ipsilateral upper BPP), Horner syndrome (lower BPP/T1), clavicle fracture, posterior humeral dislocation (US early)
6.3 Management Algorithm

Brachial Plexus Palsy — Management Timeline (Smith JAMA Pediatr 2018)
Birth: thorough exam — SCM (contracture/pseudotumor), fractures (clavicle/humerus/ribs), abdominal asymmetry (hemidiaphragm palsy), ocular asymmetry (Horner). US early if posterior humeral dislocation suspected. Refer OT immediately (early passive ROM — not immobilization): shoulder rotation; elbow/wrist flex-extend; finger flex-extend; thumb abduction. 1 month: re-evaluate; refer multidisciplinary brachial plexus palsy program if not fully recovered. 6 months: if no bicep strength to bring hand to mouth against gravity → SURGICAL CANDIDATE. Primary nerve surgery: nerve grafting, nerve transplant or combination → up to 90% useful function above elbow; 50–70% below elbow. Prognosis: 93–95% recover with conservative management alone.
6.4 Phrenic Nerve Paralysis
Etiology: difficult breech delivery → lateral hyperextension neck → overstretching/avulsion of C3–C5 (phrenic nerve). Almost always unilateral + ipsilateral upper BPP
Clinical: recurrent cyanosis + labored thoracic breathing; absent abdominal thrust on inspiration; breath sounds ↓ affected side
CXR: elevation of affected hemidiaphragm + heart/mediastinum displacement to opposite side + bilateral atelectasis. Bedside US: abnormal motion of hemidiaphragm (preferred initial diagnostic tool)

Treatment: position on involved side + oxygen. Plication: thoracoscopic (current) vs thoracotomy. Timing controversy: de Vries → wait 30 days (allow spontaneous recovery); Rizeq J Pediatr Surg 2020 → plication <30 days → earlier ventilator wean + shorter stay + ↓ readmissions
Prognosis: if avulsion → cannot spontaneously recover. Surgical plication → no recurrent pneumonia, no late complications
7. Neck, Clavicle, and Skeletal Injuries
7.1 Clavicle Fracture — Most Common Neonatal Fracture
Most common fracture during delivery; most often greenstick type. Causes: difficult delivery of shoulders (vertex), extended arms (breech); may occur after apparently normal delivery

Greenstick: often asymptomatic → first detected as callus at 7–10 days. Complete fracture: immobility affected arm, deformity/discoloration, crepitus, absent ipsilateral Moro reflex; confirm radiograph
DDx: humerus fracture, brachial palsy, congenital pseudoarthrosis (painless lump + no mobility limitation)
Treatment: immobilization: arm abducted >60°, elbow flexed >90°. Pain subsides 7–10 days (callus forms). Prognosis: excellent — restoration of normal contour within months
7.2 Humerus and Femur Fractures

Humerus: second most common long bone fracture; breech + vertex delivery. Usually diaphysis; greenstick or complete. Immobilization adduction 2–4 wk. Prognosis: excellent; complete union 3 wk; overgrowth expected → fair alignment + shortening <1 inch = satisfactory

Femur: most common lower extremity fracture; breech delivery (pulling one leg). Obvious thigh deformity, refuses to move leg. Treatment: traction-suspension both lower extremities (even if unilateral) → spica cast 3–4 wk (Bryant traction). Prognosis: excellent — complete union + restoration without shortening
7.3 Congenital Muscular Torticollis (SCM Injury)

Hard, fusiform SCM mass 1–2 cm; first noted 10–14 days; no inflammation; head tilts toward involved side, chin elevated and rotated to opposite shoulder. Mass enlarges 2–4 wk then slowly regresses by 5–8 months
Mechanism (Davids J Pediatr Orthop 1993): intrauterine/perinatal compartment syndrome from kinking (not tearing) of SCM → ischemia → compartment tamponade → fibrosis

Treatment: early gentle stretching (head flexed forward + rotated toward affected side) several times daily by mother; crib positioned to encourage spontaneous overcorrection. If uncorrected by 6 months → consider surgery (tenotomy/lengthening/excision) before permanent skull asymmetry
Prognosis: most infants → complete recovery 2–3 months with conservative therapy. Surgery before age 1 → facial asymmetry disappears almost entirely
8. Spinal Cord Injuries
8.1 Clinical Patterns (4 Types)

rows: Catastrophic: stillborn or poor condition from birth; respiratory depression; no movement; no reflexes; shock; hypothermia — high cervical/brainstem lesion
Initially normal or similar to Group 1; cardiac function strong. Flaccidity + leg immobility apparent days later; urinary retention; brachial plexus 20%
Death after several days (pneumonia in atelectatic lung)
C7–T1 or lower; paraplegia noted at birth (may be transient). Long survivors (years); paraplegia-in-flexion (later reflex stage); neurogenic bladder/bowel; skin breakdown; UTI
Subtle spasticity — thought to be cerebral palsy; partial injury ± CVA

blocks.text: 8.2 Diagnosis และ Management
MRI: gold standard for spinal cord; hemorrhage → poor prognosis; extensive edema without hemorrhage → complete recovery possible. Ultrasonography: bedside, cord edema/hematomyelia assessment

Bladder management: indwelling catheter within hours after severe cord trauma. After paraplegia-in-flexion stage: intermittent catheterization; voiding cystourethrogram; urodynamics if abnormal. Neurogenic bladder: oxybutynin (anticholinergic) + CIC q3–4h
Bowel: glycerin suppositories q regular intervals; digital manipulation PRN; soft stools by diet
Skin: dry + scaly skin → decubitus ulcers → meticulous turning and skin care

Novel therapies (case reports): surgical repair of cervical fracture-dislocation (Saleh J Neurosurg Pediatr 2018); therapeutic hypothermia for spinal cord hemorrhage (Montaldo Arch Dis Child 2016) → neurologically normal at 1 year except unilateral arm weakness

MacKinnon prognostic algorithm J Pediatr 1993: age at first breath + rate of recovery of breathing + limb movement at 3 wk → rapid recovery → prognosis clear by 3 wk; no recovery breathing/extremities by 3 months → universally poor outcome
9. Intra-Abdominal Birth Injuries

Intra-Abdominal Injury — Suspect When
Suspect in ANY newborn with: shock + abdominal distension OR pallor + anemia + irritability WITHOUT evidence of external blood loss. Predisposing history: macrosomia, breech delivery, hepatomegaly (erythroblastosis, IDM), dystocia, asphyxia, coagulopathy. Order: abdomen US → CT → paracentesis (if US equivocal) → laparotomy (definitive).
9.1 Liver Rupture — Most Common Abdominal Injury
Autopsy incidence: 0.9–9.6%; usually subcapsular hematoma rather than true laceration

Clinical: appears normal day 1–3 (sometimes up to 7 days). Nonspecific: poor feeding, pallor, jaundice, tachycardia. Hematocrit drops serially. Then SUDDEN circulatory collapse (hematoma ruptures through capsule → hemoperitoneum). Abdomen distended, rigid, dull; bluish overlying skin; may extend to scrotum
Imaging: US: echogenic fresh hemorrhage → evolves echolucent. CT: subcapsular hemorrhage without rupture. CXR: liver enlargement, NG tube displaced, uniform opacity (hemoperitoneum)
Treatment: transfuse pRBC + correct coagulopathy. Role of surgery controversial: tamponade of intra-abdominal pressure + transfusion may be adequate (hemostasis difficult at surgery)
9.2 Splenic Rupture
Less common than liver. ↑ risk: large infants, breech, erythroblastosis fetalis, congenital syphilis (enlarged, friable spleen), coagulopathy

Treatment: pRBC + correct clotting → immediate exploratory laparotomy. Every attempt to repair + preserve spleen (prevent ↑ infection risk post-splenectomy). Gel Foam + Surgicel packing. Prognosis: with early recognition + emergency surgery → survival rate approaches 100%
9.3 Adrenal Hemorrhage
More common than suspected; subclinical hemorrhage common at autopsy. Risk: macrosomia, DM, breech, syphilis, dystocia
Classic signs: fever, tachypnea out of proportion to fever, yellowish pallor, flank mass + overlying discoloration. Adrenal insufficiency signs: poor feeding, vomiting, diarrhea, hypoglycemia, shock
Scrotal hematoma presentation: must distinguish from testicular torsion → Doppler US to confirm testicular blood flow
US serial: solid → cystic (3–5 day intervals) = adrenal hemorrhage evolution. Calcification by day 12; complete calcification weeks later (adrenal gland shape)
DDx: neuroblastoma (solid on serial US; ↑ VMA + urinary catecholamines in 85–90%; persistent) vs adrenal hemorrhage (evolves cystic)
Treatment: pRBC transfusion; corticosteroids if adrenal insufficiency suspected; surgery if hemoperitoneum or blood loss exceeds replacement
Follow-up: ACTH stimulation test after discharge to assess adrenal function
9.4 Renal Injury
Rare; same predisposing factors. Signs: hematuria, flank mass, ascites, hemoperitoneum. Doppler US: assess renal arterial/venous flow. Surgery: nephrectomy if no renal blood flow
Pre-existing renal anomaly (e.g., hydronephrosis) → enhanced risk
10. NICU Practical — การเตรียมรับและจัดการ Birth Injuries
10.1 การรับทารกที่มี Subgaleal Hemorrhage
Immediate: admit NICU; serial Hct + HR + BP q1h; measure head circumference every 2–4h (↑ 1 cm = ~38 mL blood)
Volume resuscitation: pRBC + FFP; target Hct >35%; fibrinogen >100 if DIC. หากมี DIC → FFP + cryoprecipitate + platelet
ระวัง 'silent presentation': ถ้า HR ↑ + pale + Hct ↓ หลัง vacuum delivery แม้ไม่มี scalp mass ชัด → treat as subgaleal hemorrhage จนกว่าจะ rule out
10.2 การดูแล Brachial Plexus Palsy
Day 1: assess carefully (SCM, clavicle/humerus/ribs, diaphragm function, Horner). US ไหล่หา posterior humeral dislocation early
OT referral ทันที: passive ROM ไม่ใช่ immobilization — elbow/wrist ROM วันละหลายครั้ง
1 เดือน: ถ้าไม่ fully recover → refer to multidisciplinary brachial plexus palsy program
6 เดือน: ถ้า bicep ไม่แข็งแรงพอยก hand to mouth against gravity → nerve surgery
Ipsilateral phrenic palsy: ประเมิน diaphragm ด้วย bedside US; ถ้าต้อง ventilator → plication timing per team
10.3 Subcutaneous Fat Necrosis — Follow-Up Protocol
Monitor serum calcium WEEKLY สัปดาห์ที่ 3–6 หลังคลอด: hypercalcemia onset 3–4 wk
ถ้า Ca ↑: IV hydration + furosemide; ถ้า severe → bisphosphonate (pamidronate IV); ยา 1,25-OH vitamin D → ลด if giving bisphosphonate
Inform family: lesions appear 'scary' but benign — soften 6–8 wk, regress months; monitor Ca = key concern
10.4 Intra-Abdominal Injury Protocol
Shock + abdominal distension = emergency: IV access × 2, pRBC, FFP, surgical consult stat
Liver: US first; serial Hct; do NOT assume stable. Rupture through capsule = sudden decompensation → OR immediately
Adrenal: serial US q3–5 days; scrotal hematoma → Doppler US (r/o testicular torsion before unnecessary surgery)
10.5 Documentation Protocol — ป้องกัน Child Abuse Suspicion
Document ทุก birth injury ใน chart before discharge: ระบุ lesion, location, size, timing, mechanism. Photographs ถ้าทำได้
Retinal hemorrhages: must be specifically documented — otherwise ภายหลังอาจถูกตีความว่า shaken baby
Rib fractures: document ทันที — rib fractures ใน infant = high suspicion child abuse หากไม่มี birth history
Femur fracture at unusual presentation: document timing carefully ± กำหนด prenatal vs delivery vs postnatal (Senanayake J Obstet 2003: midtrimester fracture with no maternal trauma)
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Subgaleal = 'Silent Killer': Monitor Hct + Head Circumference ไม่ใช่รอ Mass
Subgaleal space จุได้ >250 mL — ทั้ง neonatal blood volume. 14% mortality (Swanson Acta Obstet 2012). 'Silent presentation': fluctuant mass ไม่ชัดเจน แต่ Hct ↓ + HR ↑ หลัง vacuum delivery = treat as subgaleal hemorrhage. Measure head circumference serially: 1 cm increase ≈ 38 mL blood lost. ทารกที่ stable พอรับไป well-baby nursery: ยังต้อง close monitoring — สามารถ decompensate หลังจากนั้นได้.

PEARL 2 — Subcutaneous Fat Necrosis: ระวัง Ca ที่ 3–4 สัปดาห์
ทารกที่มี fat necrosis (จาก hypothermia, asphyxia) มักดู normal ก่อน — แล้ว present ด้วย hypercalcemia (Ca 17+ mg/dL) ที่ 3–4 wk. Mechanism: granulomatous cells ผลิต 1,25(OH)₂D extrarenally. อาการ: vomiting, somnolence, irritability. Treatment: furosemide + IV hydration; pamidronate ถ้า severe. Monitor Ca weekly สัปดาห์ที่ 3–6. Inform family ล่วงหน้าก่อน discharge.

PEARL 3 — Brachial Plexus: Surgery Window = 6 Months (Bicep Test)
ถ้า refer ช้าเกิน 6 เดือน → nerve surgery window ปิด → permanent deficit. Criteria: cannot bring hand to mouth against gravity ที่ 6 months → surgical candidate. Early OT (passive ROM ทันที — ไม่ใช่ immobilize) + multidisciplinary referral ที่ 1 เดือนถ้าไม่ recover. 93–95% recover conservative; ที่เหลือ → nerve grafting/transplant → 90% useful function above elbow, 50–70% below elbow.

PEARL 4 — Intra-Abdominal Injury: Shock Without External Blood Loss = Suspect Internal
Liver rupture present ปกติ day 1–3 แล้ว SUDDEN shock เมื่อ hematoma ruptures capsule. ไม่มี external bleeding → ลืม consider ไม่ได้. Suspect เมื่อ: macrosomic, breech, IDM, erythroblastosis, asphyxia + shock + abdominal distension + ↓ Hct. US liver/spleen/adrenal stat; serial Hct q1–2h. Adrenal hemorrhage → serial US q3–5d (solid→cystic = adrenal; persistent solid = neuroblastoma).

PEARL 5 — Document Everything Before Discharge: Child Abuse Prevention
Retinal hemorrhages: ถ้าไม่ document ตั้งแต่ newborn period → เมื่อพบภายหลัง อาจถูกสงสัยว่า shaken baby syndrome. Rib fractures: ถ้าไม่มี birth documentation → high suspicion of non-accidental trauma. Femur fractures, ecchymoses, scalp injuries: document location, size, mechanism, timing. Photographs ควรเก็บใน chart. การ document คือ medicolegal protection ทั้งของทารก, ครอบครัว, และทีมแพทย์.
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Swanson AE et al Acta Obstet Gynecol Scand 2012;91:260: subgaleal hemorrhage: mortality 14%; risk factors: vacuum extraction, male sex, coagulopathy, macrosomia
Levin G et al Eur J Obstet Gynecol Reprod Biol 2020;245:205: factors associated with severity of neonatal subgaleal hemorrhage after vacuum-assisted delivery
Falco NA & Eriksson E Plast Reconstr Surg 1990;85:1: facial nerve palsy in newborn: 90% complete spontaneous recovery; comprehensive management algorithm
Hogeling M et al Pediatr Dermatol 2012;29:59: extensive subcutaneous fat necrosis associated with therapeutic hypothermia — important complication of cooling therapy
Samedi VM et al Am J Perinatol Reports 2014;4:e93: neonatal hypercalcemia secondary to subcutaneous fat necrosis: pamidronate case series + review — successful short-term treatment
Smith BW et al JAMA Pediatr 2018;172:585: update on management of neonatal brachial plexus palsy: replacing immobilization paradigm with early OT + surgery decision algorithm
Vakhshori V et al J Pediatr Orthopaed B 2020;29:392: risk factors associated with neonatal brachial plexus palsy in the US: shoulder dystocia, macrosomia, malpresentation
Curtis C et al J Hand Surg Am 2002;27:470: Active Movement Scale: evaluative tool for infants with obstetrical BPP — subthreshold at 3 and 9 months predicts need for primary nerve surgery
Rizeq YK et al J Pediatr Surg 2020;55:240: diaphragmatic paralysis after phrenic nerve injury in newborns: plication before 30 days → earlier extubation, shorter stay, fewer readmissions
Davids JR et al J Pediatr Orthop 1993;13:141: congenital muscular torticollis: intrauterine/perinatal compartment syndrome mechanism — kinking not tearing of SCM
MacKinnon JA et al J Pediatr 1993;122:431: spinal cord injury at birth: diagnostic + prognostic algorithm; rapid recovery → prognosis clear 3 wk; no recovery by 3 months → universally poor
Mills JF et al J Pediatr 2001;138:105: upper cervical spinal cord injury neonates: MRI hemorrhage → poor prognosis; extensive edema without hemorrhage → complete recovery possible
Saleh S et al J Neurosurg Pediatr 2018;21:16: successful surgical repair in 2-week-old with birth-related cervical fracture-dislocation → significant neurologic recovery
Montaldo P et al Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2016;101:F468: therapeutic hypothermia in neonatal cervical spine hemorrhagic contusion → neurologically normal at 1 year
Alexander JM et al Obstet Gynecol 2006;108:885: fetal injury associated with cesarean delivery: 1.1% incidence (13 university centers); skin lacerations 64% of all injuries
Petrikovsky BM et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 1998;179:906: cephalhematoma and caput succedaneum identified prenatally before labor onset — 5/7 had PPROM → fetal head compression vs oligohydramnios
Mangurten HH et al Neonat Intensive Care 1999;12:15: incidental rib fractures in neonate: compression forces from shoulder dystocia — fractures on posterior shoulder side
Share JC et al Pediatr Radiol 1990;20:320: unsuspected hepatic injury in neonate: ultrasound diagnosis — echogenic fresh blood, evolves echolucent with involution
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: แยก caput succedaneum vs cephalhematoma vs subgaleal hemorrhage อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Caput: above periosteum — crosses suture lines, present at birth, soft, resolves days. Cephalhematoma: subperiosteal — does NOT cross sutures (periosteal limit), not apparent for hours/days, fluctuant with ridge, resolves 3–4 wk (calcification possible). Subgaleal: between galea + periosteum — crosses EVERYTHING, diffuse, pitting edema + ear displaced anteriorly + periorbital swelling, can hold entire blood volume (>250 mL), mortality 14%. Key: subgaleal crosses sutures (like caput) but has mass effect + hemodynamic compromise.
Q: ทารกหลัง vacuum delivery มี Hct ลดลง แต่ไม่มี scalp mass ชัดเจน — ควรทำอะไร?

ตอบ: 'Silent presentation' ของ subgaleal hemorrhage — fluctuant mass อาจไม่ปรากฏแม้ serial exam. Treat as subgaleal hemorrhage จนกว่าจะ rule out: (1) admit NICU; (2) serial Hct + HR + BP q1h; (3) measure head circumference q2–4h (1 cm = ~38 mL blood); (4) volume resuscitation: pRBC + FFP; (5) ถ้า DIC: cryoprecipitate + platelet. ไม่รอ mass ปรากฏ — ถ้ารอ อาจ decompensate เร็วมาก.
Q: Subcutaneous fat necrosis: เมื่อไหร่ควร monitor calcium และทำไม?

ตอบ: Monitor serum Ca weekly สัปดาห์ที่ 3–6 หลังคลอด. Reason: granulomatous cells ใน fat necrosis ผลิต 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D extrarenally → hypercalcemia onset 3–4 wk (Ca up to 17.3 mg/dL) → vomiting, somnolence, seizures, nephrocalcinosis. Treatment: IV hydration + furosemide + potassium citrate; corticosteroids; pamidronate ถ้า severe. Risk groups: perinatal asphyxia, therapeutic hypothermia, prostaglandin E, obstetric trauma.
Q: Erb-Duchenne palsy: clinical picture คืออะไร และเมื่อไหร่ต้องส่ง surgical?

ตอบ: Erb-Duchenne (C5–C6): arm adducted + internally rotated + elbow extended + forearm pronated + wrist flexed ('waiter's tip position'). Absent: Moro, biceps, radial reflex. Grasp intact. Surgical criteria: ไม่สามารถยก hand to mouth against gravity ที่ 6 months of age → surgical candidate (nerve grafting/transplant). ถ้าไม่ fully recover ที่ 1 เดือน → refer multidisciplinary BPP program ทันที. 93–95% recover conservative management.
Q: Intra-abdominal injury หลัง breech delivery: present อย่างไร และ most common organ คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Most common: liver (hepatic rupture) — autopsy incidence 0.9–9.6%. Pattern: ทารกดูปกติวัน 1–3 → sudden circulatory collapse (subcapsular hematoma ruptures → hemoperitoneum). Before rupture: poor feeding, pallor, jaundice, tachycardia, serial ↓ Hct, RUQ mass. After: abdominal distension + rigidity + dullness to percussion + bluish discoloration. Suspect ใน: macrosomic, IDM, erythroblastosis, breech, asphyxia, coagulopathy. Management: US → pRBC + FFP → surgical consult.
Q: ทำไม birth injuries ทุกอย่างต้อง document ก่อน discharge?

ตอบ: ป้องกัน inappropriate suspicion of child abuse ภายหลัง. Examples: (1) retinal hemorrhages ถ้าไม่มี birth documentation → suspicious ว่า shaken baby (abusive head trauma); (2) rib fractures ใน infant = classic child abuse finding ถ้าไม่มี birth record; (3) skull fractures, clavicle fractures, femur fractures ใน unexpected location/age → forensic problem. Document: lesion, location, size, mechanism, timing — photographs ใน chart. นี่คือ medicolegal protection ของทุกฝ่าย.
Q: Phrenic nerve paralysis: diagnose อย่างไร และ timing ของ surgical plication?

ตอบ: Diagnosis: clinical (thoracic breathing, absent abdominal thrust, ↓ breath sounds affected side) + bedside US diaphragm: abnormal motion (preferred); CXR: elevated hemidiaphragm + mediastinal shift; fluoroscopy (equivocal cases). Surgical plication timing: de Vries → wait 30 days (allow spontaneous recovery); Rizeq J Pediatr Surg 2020 → plication <30 days → earlier ventilator wean + shorter hospital stay + ↓ readmissions. ถ้า nerve avulsion → cannot recover spontaneously → surgery essential.
Q: Cephalhematoma infected: คิดได้อย่างไร และ complications มีอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: Suspect เมื่อ: sudden enlargement of static cephalhematoma during systemic infection; relapse meningitis/sepsis despite antibiotics; local signs of infection (erythema, warmth) overlying. Routine aspiration CONTRAINDICATED (infection risk). Diagnostic aspiration when infection suspected. Complications of infected cephalhematoma: osteomyelitis of underlying skull (CT for permeative bone erosion); venous sinus thrombosis; cerebellar hemorrhage → MRI for intracranial complications.
Q: Adrenal hemorrhage vs neuroblastoma: แยกอย่างไรใน newborn?

ตอบ: Adrenal hemorrhage: solid → evolves to cystic on serial US (q3–5 days) as clot lyses; VMA + urinary catecholamines normal. Neuroblastoma: persistent solid lesion on serial US + ↑ VMA + urinary catecholamines (85–90% of cases). Key imaging: US จาก solid → cystic = diagnostic for adrenal hemorrhage. Adrenal hemorrhage typical history: macrosomic + breech + traumatic delivery. ถ้า solid lesion ไม่ evolve → workup neuroblastoma (VMA, CT, MIBG scan).

Day 126 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.29
บทต่อไป: Chapter 29 — Congenital Anomalies. เตรียมตัว: approach ต่อ multiple congenital anomalies; chromosomal syndromes (Trisomy 13, 18, 21) — clinical features + prognosis; VACTERL association; CHARGE syndrome; structural anomalies detected by prenatal US vs postnatal; congenital heart disease ที่พบร่วมกับ syndromes; genetic counseling approach ใน delivery room; palliative care considerations ใน lethal anomalies.
